works.name: Kanyō
works.tech_stack: Python (opencv-python-headless, ultralytics, numpy, Pillow, yt-dlp, pyyaml, loguru, requests, jinja2, markdown, python-dateutil, tzdata)
works.short_description: Production computer vision pipeline for wildlife monitoring. Streams from YouTube (or any rtsp/http source), runs YOLOv8 bird detection with a debounced state machine to suppress false positives, records arrival/departure clips, sends Telegram notifications. Companion web viewer provides a public event archive with HKSV-style timeline, visit statistics, live stream embeds. Deployed at Harvard's Memorial Hall monitoring peregrine falcons, developed for Nobel laureate economist Claudia Goldin and Harvard FAS. Species-agnostic architecture. Built from zero development experience in six weeks using the Ho System. 114 passing tests.
substantive_description: Kanyō is the existence proof. The methodology question — can a practitioner with strong systems thinking but no formal engineering background ship production AI? — needed an answer that wasn't a position paper. Six weeks. 114 tests. Deployed at Harvard, monitoring peregrine falcons through an academic partnership with a Nobel laureate. The system is real, and it works.

Beyond the personal evidence, Kanyō makes a more interesting argument: the kind of work this is — domain-specific computer vision with research-grade requirements — has historically required either a specialist engineering team or compromise on quality. The Ho System collapses the dichotomy. The deployment context matters: a peregrine falcon is not a benchmark. Harvard FAS is not a test environment. The system runs in production for a researcher who needed it to work. That's the evidence the methodology needed.

relationships.note: Six weeks, 114 tests, deployed at Harvard — the methodology's first existence proof.

state.last: The bulk of the `021` sprint: a sequence of agent-task-driven fixes across admin timezone handling (021-G), arrival confirmation recording actual last detection time (021-J), buffer monitor startup / frame interval wiring (021-D), .mp4.tmp finalization in roosting-stop mode (021-E), stream data root unification (021-C), HTML/URL escaping in admin API handlers (021-H), admin restart/stop/start TypeError on thumbnail (021-B), EventMetadata/StateEvent type aliases bringing mypy to clean (021-K), pytest import without manual PYTHONPATH (021-A), CI build-cpu tag template fix (5557794), and output filename microsecond collision fix (021-I, commit a014025 — most recent merged). The ho-09 significance-filter doc was scaffolded (1e69053) but the ho is not yet authored or executed.
state.next: Author and execute ho-09 (significance filter / event merging). The scaffold and motivating framing are in `devlog/ho-09-significance-filter.md`. Several Agent Tasks (013–020) also appear open in `devlog/Agent-Tasks/` and may be sequenced before or alongside ho-09; the practitioner should confirm priority on the next session.

Status of these tasks is uncertain from git history alone — practitioner to verify which are still active vs. superseded. No build blockers identified from evidence.
